# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the order matching engine.

The "nouns" of the exchange live here: enums, the typed exception family,
the immutable Trade record, the mutable Order entity, and the OrderBook for
ONE symbol (the Information Expert that owns price-time matching via two heaps).
"""

import heapq
import itertools
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum


# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A trader either wants to BUY or SELL. This is the single most important field
# on an order — it decides which side of the book the order rests on and which
# side it can match against. We NEVER collapse it into a bool; a named side is
# what the interviewer wants to see survive end to end.
# 🎙️ "Side is BUY or SELL. A buy can only fill against asks, a sell against bids."
class Side(Enum):
    BUY = "BUY"
    SELL = "SELL"


# ── WHY THIS ENUM ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# An order moves through a tiny lifecycle. We mark it explicitly so a caller can
# ask "did my order rest, partially fill, fully fill, or get cancelled?" without
# re-deriving it from quantities.
class OrderStatus(Enum):
    OPEN = "OPEN"                  # resting on the book, nothing filled yet
    PARTIALLY_FILLED = "PARTIAL"   # some quantity filled, remainder resting
    FILLED = "FILLED"              # fully filled, no longer on the book
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"        # cancelled by the trader, remainder removed


# ── WHY ONE BASE EXCEPTION ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
# One base class for every failure this domain can raise. Callers can catch
# ExchangeError to handle "anything the engine rejected" and still pattern-match
# on the specific subclass. We NEVER raise a bare ValueError/RuntimeError from
# our own code — a typed error tells the caller exactly what went wrong.
class ExchangeError(Exception):
    """Base class for every error the matching engine raises."""


class UnknownSymbolError(ExchangeError):
    """A symbol was referenced that was never registered on the engine."""


class InvalidOrderError(ExchangeError):
    """An order failed validation — bad price, bad quantity, or bad side."""


class UnknownOrderError(ExchangeError):
    """A cancel referenced an order id the engine has never seen."""


class OrderNotCancellableError(ExchangeError):
    """A cancel hit an order that is already filled or already cancelled."""


# ── WHY THIS VALUE OBJECT ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A Trade is the FACT that a buy and a sell met: who, how much, at what price.
# It never changes once printed, so it is frozen — once a trade is recorded the
# exchange has made a promise it cannot edit. Price is integer CENTS, never a
# float, because money rounding errors are unacceptable on an exchange.
# 🎙️ "A Trade is immutable — once two orders match, that print is permanent."
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Trade:
    trade_id: str
    symbol: str
    buy_order_id: str
    sell_order_id: str
    price_cents: int               # the resting order's price sets the trade price
    quantity: int                  # shares exchanged in THIS fill (may be partial)


# ── WHY THIS ENTITY ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# An Order is the live thing on the book. It owns its OWN remaining quantity and
# its OWN status — the book asks it to fill, it does the arithmetic on itself.
# It is mutable because remaining_qty shrinks as it fills; that is its whole job.
# 🎙️ "The Order tracks how much of it is left. When I fill it, IT decrements
#      its own remaining and flips its own status — no one reaches in and edits it."
@dataclass
class Order:
    order_id: str
    symbol: str
    side: Side
    price_cents: int               # limit price — integer cents
    quantity: int                  # original requested quantity (never changes)
    sequence: int                  # arrival rank — the TIME in price-time priority
    remaining_qty: int = field(init=False)
    status: OrderStatus = field(default=OrderStatus.OPEN, init=False)

    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
        # remaining starts equal to the full order; it only ever shrinks.
        self.remaining_qty = self.quantity

    @property
    def filled_qty(self) -> int:
        # How much has been filled so far. Derived, never stored twice.
        return self.quantity - self.remaining_qty

    @property
    def is_active(self) -> bool:
        # Active means it still has shares that could match or rest.
        return self.status in (OrderStatus.OPEN, OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED)

    def fill(self, qty: int) -> None:
        # WHY: the order mutates ITSELF when filled. The book computes how many
        #      shares trade, then tells the order "you lost this many". The order
        #      guards its own invariant: you can never fill more than remains.
        if qty <= 0 or qty > self.remaining_qty:
            raise InvalidOrderError(
                f"cannot fill {qty} of order {self.order_id} "
                f"with {self.remaining_qty} remaining"
            )
        self.remaining_qty -= qty

        if self.remaining_qty == 0:
            self.status = OrderStatus.FILLED
        else:
            self.status = OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED

    def cancel(self) -> None:
        # WHY: cancelling just flips status. The book stops handing the order out
        #      once it is no longer active. We do NOT physically rip it out of the
        #      heap here — that is O(n); instead matching skips cancelled orders
        #      lazily when they reach the top. This keeps cancel O(1).
        self.status = OrderStatus.CANCELLED


# ── WHY THIS HELPER ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# A heap of orders needs a strict ordering key. We never put the Order object
# itself on the heap (two orders are never "less than" each other); instead we
# push a tuple whose first elements ARE the price-time priority, and carry the
# order as payload. _HeapEntry makes that tuple comparison explicit and total.
@dataclass(order=True)
class _HeapEntry:
    sort_key: tuple[int, int]      # (price component, sequence) — compared first
    order: Order = field(compare=False)   # payload, never compared


# ── WHY THIS CLASS (THE INFORMATION EXPERT) ──────────────────────────────────
# The OrderBook owns ALL matching for ONE symbol. It is the Information Expert:
# it holds both sides, so it is the only thing that can decide whether two orders
# cross and in what order. The engine never reaches inside the book to match —
# it hands the book a new order and the book returns the trades.
#
# Two heaps give price-time priority in O(log n):
#   - bids: a MAX-heap on price (highest buyer first). Python heapq is a MIN-heap,
#     so we store -price to flip it.
#   - asks: a MIN-heap on price (lowest seller first) — heapq's natural order.
# Within one price, the heap's second key (sequence) breaks ties by EARLIEST
# arrival — that is the "time" in price-time priority, and it is FIFO.
# 🎙️ "The book is two heaps. Best bid is the highest price; best ask is the
#      lowest price. They cross when the best bid is at least the best ask."
class OrderBook:
    def __init__(self, symbol: str) -> None:
        self.symbol = symbol
        self._bids: list[_HeapEntry] = []     # max-heap by price (store -price)
        self._asks: list[_HeapEntry] = []     # min-heap by price
        # id → Order, so cancel can find an order and flip its status in O(1).
        self._orders_by_id: dict[str, Order] = {}

    # ── best-of-book peeks (skip lazily-deleted dead orders) ──────────────────

    def _peek_active(self, heap: list[_HeapEntry]) -> Order | None:
        # WHY: cancelled or fully-filled orders are left in the heap (lazy delete)
        #      and popped off the top only when we look. We discard dead tops here
        #      so the caller always sees a live best order or None.
        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
            heapq.heappop(heap)

        return heap[0].order if heap else None

    def best_bid(self) -> Order | None:
        return self._peek_active(self._bids)

    def best_ask(self) -> Order | None:
        return self._peek_active(self._asks)

    # ── matching (the heart) ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def match(self, incoming: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]") -> list[Trade]:
        # WHY: a SINGLE incoming order may sweep MANY resting orders on the other
        #      side, generating several trades, then rest its remainder. All of
        #      that happens here in one call so the book never tears — the engine
        #      runs this whole method under one per-symbol lock.
        self._orders_by_id[incoming.order_id] = incoming
        trades: list[Trade] = []

        opposite = self._asks if incoming.side is Side.BUY else self._bids

        while incoming.remaining_qty > 0:
            resting = self._peek_active(opposite)

            if resting is None or not self._crosses(incoming, resting):
                break

            trades.append(self._execute(incoming, resting, trade_ids))

        if incoming.remaining_qty > 0 and incoming.is_active:
            self._rest(incoming)

        return trades

    def _crosses(self, incoming: Order, resting: Order) -> bool:
        # WHY: a buy crosses a sell when the buyer will pay AT LEAST what the
        #      seller asks. A sell crosses a bid when the seller accepts AT MOST
        #      what the buyer offers. This is the "limit" in limit order.
        if incoming.side is Side.BUY:
            return incoming.price_cents >= resting.price_cents

        return incoming.price_cents <= resting.price_cents

    def _execute(
        self, incoming: Order, resting: Order, trade_ids: "itertools.count[int]"
    ) -> Trade:
        # WHY: the fill quantity is the smaller of the two remaining sizes — that
        #      is what makes a fill PARTIAL. The trade prints at the RESTING
        #      order's price (price-time priority: the order that waited sets the
        #      price, so the aggressor never gets a worse price than they asked).
        qty = min(incoming.remaining_qty, resting.remaining_qty)
        price = resting.price_cents

        incoming.fill(qty)
        resting.fill(qty)

        buy_id = incoming.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else resting.order_id
        sell_id = resting.order_id if incoming.side is Side.BUY else incoming.order_id

        return Trade(
            trade_id=f"trade-{next(trade_ids)}",
            symbol=self.symbol,
            buy_order_id=buy_id,
            sell_order_id=sell_id,
            price_cents=price,
            quantity=qty,
        )

    def _rest(self, order: Order) -> None:
        # WHY: whatever did not match goes onto the book to wait. The sort key is
        #      (price, sequence). For bids we negate price so the MAX price sits
        #      at the heap top; for asks the natural MIN price sits on top. The
        #      sequence is the tie-breaker = earliest arrival wins at one price.
        if order.side is Side.BUY:
            key = (-order.price_cents, order.sequence)
            heapq.heappush(self._bids, _HeapEntry(key, order))
        else:
            key = (order.price_cents, order.sequence)
            heapq.heappush(self._asks, _HeapEntry(key, order))

    # ── cancel ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def cancel(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        # WHY: look the order up by id, then ask IT to cancel itself. We do not
        #      remove it from the heap now (O(n)); matching skips it lazily.
        order = self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)

        if order is None:
            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found on {self.symbol}")

        if not order.is_active:
            raise OrderNotCancellableError(
                f"order {order_id} is {order.status.value}, cannot cancel"
            )

        order.cancel()
        return order

    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order | None:
        return self._orders_by_id.get(order_id)

    # ── invariant checks (used by tests and the demo) ─────────────────────────

    def is_crossed(self) -> bool:
        # WHY: a healthy book is NEVER crossed — the best bid must be strictly
        #      below the best ask after matching settles. If best_bid >= best_ask
        #      we left money on the table (two orders that should have traded did
        #      not). The concurrency test asserts this is False.
        bid = self.best_bid()
        ask = self.best_ask()

        if bid is None or ask is None:
            return False

        return bid.price_cents >= ask.price_cents

    def resting_quantity(self, side: Side) -> int:
        # WHY: total live shares resting on one side. Used to prove conservation:
        #      shares submitted == shares filled + shares still resting.
        heap = self._bids if side is Side.BUY else self._asks
        return sum(e.order.remaining_qty for e in heap if e.order.is_active)

# Python 3.11+
"""The matching engine orchestrator.

`MatchingEngine` is the clean, single-threaded core. It owns one OrderBook per
symbol and a global id->symbol index so a cancel can find any order. The public
methods (`submit_order`, `cancel_order`, `get_order`, `snapshot`) are the entire
external surface.

Concurrency is a deliberate SECOND version: `ThreadSafeMatchingEngine` overrides
ONLY the `_lock` hook to take a PER-SYMBOL reentrant lock, so different symbols
match in parallel while everything touching ONE book serialises. The core file
below has zero lock code.
"""

import itertools
import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    ExchangeError,
    InvalidOrderError,
    Order,
    OrderBook,
    Side,
    Trade,
    UnknownOrderError,
    UnknownSymbolError,
)


# ── WHY THIS CLASS (THE ORCHESTRATOR) ────────────────────────────────────────
# The engine is the public API. It does NOT match orders itself — it routes an
# incoming order to the right symbol's OrderBook (Information Expert) and returns
# the trades. It owns the symbol->book map, the id->symbol index for cancels, and
# the monotonic counters that stamp arrival sequence (the "time" in price-time).
# 🎙️ "The engine is a thin router. The book does the matching; the engine just
#      finds the right book, gives it the order, and hands back the trades."
class MatchingEngine:
    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]) -> None:
        if not symbols:
            raise InvalidOrderError("engine needs at least one symbol")

        # symbol -> its own independent book. Different symbols share NO state,
        # which is exactly why the thread-safe version can lock per symbol.
        self._books: dict[str, OrderBook] = {s: OrderBook(s) for s in symbols}
        # order id -> symbol, so cancel_order/get_order can route without the
        # caller telling us the symbol again.
        self._order_symbol: dict[str, str] = {}
        # Numbered ids make test assertions readable; UUIDs in production.
        # One counter stamps BOTH the order id and its arrival sequence so the
        # id "ord-7" and sequence 7 always agree — easy to read in tests.
        self._order_seq = itertools.count(1)
        self._trade_seq = itertools.count(1)

    # ── concurrency hook (no-op in the clean core) ───────────────────────────

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean version — there is zero lock code in
        #      this file. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this method with
        #      a real per-symbol lock. The diff between clean and safe is ~10 lines.
        yield

    # ── public surface ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def submit_order(
        self, symbol: str, side: Side, price_cents: int, quantity: int
    ) -> tuple[Order, list[Trade]]:
        # WHY: this is the pipeline — validate, build the order, match it against
        #      the book, rest the remainder. The whole match runs under one
        #      per-symbol lock so a single submit either fully resolves or not at
        #      all; the book never tears mid-sweep.
        self._validate(symbol, price_cents, quantity)
        book = self._books[symbol]

        with self._lock(symbol):
            seq = next(self._order_seq)
            order = Order(
                order_id=f"ord-{seq}",
                symbol=symbol,
                side=side,
                price_cents=price_cents,
                quantity=quantity,
                sequence=seq,
            )
            self._order_symbol[order.order_id] = symbol
            trades = book.match(order, self._trade_seq)

        return order, trades

    def cancel_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        # WHY: route by the id index to the owning book, then cancel under that
        #      book's lock so a cancel can never race a match on the same symbol.
        symbol = self._order_symbol.get(order_id)

        if symbol is None:
            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")

        with self._lock(symbol):
            return self._books[symbol].cancel(order_id)

    def get_order(self, order_id: str) -> Order:
        # WHY: read the live order so a caller can inspect fill state. Routed via
        #      the same id index. A missing id is a typed error, not a None.
        symbol = self._order_symbol.get(order_id)

        if symbol is None:
            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")

        order = self._books[symbol].get_order(order_id)

        if order is None:
            raise UnknownOrderError(f"order {order_id} not found")

        return order

    def snapshot(self, symbol: str) -> dict[str, int | bool | None]:
        # WHY: a read-only view of one book's top of book + resting depth. Used by
        #      the demo and tests to prove the book is consistent (not crossed).
        #      This LOOKS read-only, but best_bid/best_ask/is_crossed call
        #      _peek_active, which heappop()s lazily-deleted dead tops — a MUTATION
        #      of the heap list. So a monitoring thread polling snapshot() races a
        #      submit_order() on the same symbol: two threads mutating one heap
        #      unsynchronised tears the book (IndexError under contention). We MUST
        #      take the same per-symbol lock the write path uses, so the book is
        #      only ever mutated by one thread at a time.
        if symbol not in self._books:
            raise UnknownSymbolError(f"unknown symbol {symbol}")

        book = self._books[symbol]

        with self._lock(symbol):
            bid = book.best_bid()
            ask = book.best_ask()

            return {
                "best_bid": bid.price_cents if bid else None,
                "best_ask": ask.price_cents if ask else None,
                "bid_qty": book.resting_quantity(Side.BUY),
                "ask_qty": book.resting_quantity(Side.SELL),
                "crossed": book.is_crossed(),
            }

    # ── validation helper ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    def _validate(self, symbol: str, price_cents: int, quantity: int) -> None:
        # WHY: reject bad input at the public boundary BEFORE we mutate any book.
        #      Every guard raises a specific typed error so the caller knows why.
        if symbol not in self._books:
            raise UnknownSymbolError(f"unknown symbol {symbol}")

        if price_cents <= 0:
            raise InvalidOrderError(f"price must be positive, got {price_cents}")

        if quantity <= 0:
            raise InvalidOrderError(f"quantity must be positive, got {quantity}")


# ── WHY THIS SUBCLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# The thread-safe version. It overrides ONLY the `_lock` hook — every public
# method above is inherited UNCHANGED. We keep one RLock PER SYMBOL so two threads
# trading different symbols never block each other; only threads hitting the SAME
# symbol serialise. That is the whole concurrency design in one method.
# 🎙️ "Thread-safety is a 10-line subclass. One reentrant lock per symbol. Orders
#      on different symbols match in parallel; same-symbol orders take turns."
class ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(MatchingEngine):
    def __init__(self, symbols: list[str]) -> None:
        super().__init__(symbols)
        # One lock per symbol. We build them up front (symbols are fixed at
        # construction) so there is no map-mutation race; if symbols could be
        # added at runtime we would guard this dict with its own lock too.
        self._symbol_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {
            s: threading.RLock() for s in self._books
        }

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, symbol: str) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: RLock (reentrant) not Lock — a future guarded method might call
        #      another guarded method on the same symbol; an RLock lets the same
        #      thread re-enter instead of deadlocking on itself. Per symbol, not
        #      global, so AAPL and MSFT match at the same time.
        lock = self._symbol_locks.get(symbol)

        if lock is None:
            # Defensive: an unknown symbol should already have been rejected by
            # _validate, but if it slips through we fail loud, not silently.
            raise ExchangeError(f"no lock for unknown symbol {symbol}")

        with lock:
            yield

"""Narrated demo driver for the order matching engine.

Run:  python main.py
Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK" so a green run is obvious.
Cases cover: a simple cross, a partial fill leaving a remainder resting,
price-time (FIFO) priority within a price level, cancel, validation errors,
and a tiny concurrency proof that the book stays consistent.
"""

import threading

from exchange_service import MatchingEngine, ThreadSafeMatchingEngine
from models import (
    InvalidOrderError,
    OrderNotCancellableError,
    OrderStatus,
    Side,
    UnknownOrderError,
    UnknownSymbolError,
)


def case_1_simple_cross() -> None:
    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    # A seller rests 100 @ $10.00, then a buyer crosses at $10.00.
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)
    buy, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)

    assert len(trades) == 1, trades
    assert trades[0].quantity == 100
    assert trades[0].price_cents == 1000          # prints at the resting price
    assert buy.status is OrderStatus.FILLED
    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
    assert snap["bid_qty"] == 0 and snap["ask_qty"] == 0
    assert snap["crossed"] is False
    print("Case 1 simple full cross at resting price -> OK")


def case_2_partial_fill() -> None:
    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    # Seller has only 60; buyer wants 100 -> 60 trade, 40 of the buy rests.
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=60)
    buy, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=100)

    assert len(trades) == 1 and trades[0].quantity == 60
    assert buy.status is OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED
    assert buy.remaining_qty == 40
    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
    assert snap["best_bid"] == 1000 and snap["bid_qty"] == 40   # remainder rests
    assert snap["ask_qty"] == 0
    print("Case 2 partial fill leaves remainder resting -> OK")


def case_3_price_time_priority() -> None:
    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    # Two sells at the SAME price; the EARLIER one must fill first (FIFO time).
    s_first, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=50)
    s_second, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=50)
    # A better-priced (lower) sell jumps the queue on PRICE.
    s_cheap, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=999, quantity=30)

    _, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=60)

    # Price first: the 999 sell (30) fills entirely, then the earliest 1000 sell.
    assert trades[0].sell_order_id == s_cheap.order_id and trades[0].quantity == 30
    assert trades[1].sell_order_id == s_first.order_id and trades[1].quantity == 30
    assert engine.get_order(s_first.order_id).remaining_qty == 20
    assert engine.get_order(s_second.order_id).remaining_qty == 50  # untouched
    print("Case 3 price-time priority: best price then earliest -> OK")


def case_4_cancel() -> None:
    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    resting, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=950, quantity=40)
    engine.cancel_order(resting.order_id)
    assert engine.get_order(resting.order_id).status is OrderStatus.CANCELLED

    # A crossing sell must NOT match the cancelled bid — it rests instead.
    sell, trades = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=950, quantity=40)
    assert trades == []
    assert engine.snapshot("AAPL")["ask_qty"] == 40
    print("Case 4 cancel removes order from matching -> OK")


def case_5_validation_and_errors() -> None:
    engine = MatchingEngine(["AAPL"])

    for bad_price in (0, -5):
        try:
            engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=bad_price, quantity=10)
            raise AssertionError("expected InvalidOrderError for bad price")
        except InvalidOrderError:
            pass

    try:
        engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=0)
        raise AssertionError("expected InvalidOrderError for zero quantity")
    except InvalidOrderError:
        pass

    try:
        engine.submit_order("TSLA", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownSymbolError")
    except UnknownSymbolError:
        pass

    try:
        engine.cancel_order("ord-999")
        raise AssertionError("expected UnknownOrderError")
    except UnknownOrderError:
        pass

    # Cancelling an already-filled order is rejected.
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
    buy, _ = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
    try:
        engine.cancel_order(buy.order_id)
        raise AssertionError("expected OrderNotCancellableError")
    except OrderNotCancellableError:
        pass

    print("Case 5 validation + typed errors -> OK")


def case_6_concurrency_conservation() -> None:
    # Many threads slam crossing orders on ONE symbol. We seed a big resting buy,
    # then race sells against it. No shares may be created or destroyed, and the
    # book must not be left crossed.
    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    n_threads = 40
    qty_each = 10
    # One large resting bid that can absorb everything: 40 * 10 = 400 shares.
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=n_threads * qty_each)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
    all_trades: list = []
    lock = threading.Lock()

    def seller(_i: int) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        _order, trades = engine.submit_order(
            "AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=qty_each
        )
        with lock:
            all_trades.extend(trades)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=seller, args=(i,)) for i in range(n_threads)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    filled = sum(t.quantity for t in all_trades)
    assert filled == n_threads * qty_each, f"shares not conserved: {filled}"
    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
    assert snap["crossed"] is False
    assert snap["bid_qty"] == 0 and snap["ask_qty"] == 0   # all consumed exactly
    print(f"Case 6 concurrency: {filled} shares filled, book consistent -> OK")


def case_7_per_symbol_parallelism() -> None:
    # Different symbols share no state, so they match independently.
    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL", "MSFT"])
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
    engine.submit_order("MSFT", Side.SELL, price_cents=2000, quantity=5)
    _, t_aapl = engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=10)
    _, t_msft = engine.submit_order("MSFT", Side.BUY, price_cents=2000, quantity=5)

    assert t_aapl[0].symbol == "AAPL" and t_aapl[0].price_cents == 1000
    assert t_msft[0].symbol == "MSFT" and t_msft[0].price_cents == 2000
    print("Case 7 per-symbol books are independent -> OK")


def main() -> None:
    case_1_simple_cross()
    case_2_partial_fill()
    case_3_price_time_priority()
    case_4_cancel()
    case_5_validation_and_errors()
    case_6_concurrency_conservation()
    case_7_per_symbol_parallelism()
    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

def test_concurrent_crossing_conserves_quantity() -> None:
    # N seller threads each submit a crossing SELL of qty_each on ONE symbol, all
    # released at one instant by the Barrier so they truly race the same book.
    # A single big resting BUY can absorb everything.
    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    n_threads = 50
    qty_each = 10
    total = n_threads * qty_each
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=total)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
    collected: list[Trade] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def seller() -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        _order, trades = engine.submit_order(
            "AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000, quantity=qty_each
        )
        with guard:
            collected.extend(trades)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=seller) for _ in range(n_threads)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # STRICT post-state assertions.
    buys_filled = sum(t.quantity for t in collected)
    # Conservation: sum of buy fills == sum of sell fills == total submitted sells.
    assert buys_filled == total, f"shares created/destroyed: {buys_filled} != {total}"

    # The resting buy must be exactly fully filled — not over, not under.
    big_buy = engine.get_order("ord-1")
    assert big_buy.status is OrderStatus.FILLED
    assert big_buy.remaining_qty == 0

    # Every share traded at the single crossing price.
    assert all(t.price_cents == 1000 for t in collected)

    # The book is empty and NOT crossed — no quantity left hanging.
    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
    assert snap["bid_qty"] == 0 and snap["ask_qty"] == 0
    assert snap["crossed"] is False


def test_concurrent_partial_overflow_rests_exactly_the_surplus() -> None:
    # Now the resting BUY can absorb only HALF. The surplus sells must rest, and
    # the conserved-quantity rule still holds: filled + resting == submitted.
    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    n_threads = 40
    qty_each = 10
    submitted = n_threads * qty_each            # 400 shares of sells incoming
    absorb = submitted // 2                     # resting buy takes only 200
    engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=1000, quantity=absorb)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
    collected: list[Trade] = []

        )

    # Conservation across the whole race: nothing created or destroyed.
    assert filled + resting_sells == submitted

    # Threads on DIFFERENT symbols run in parallel; each book stays independent.

    guard = threading.Lock()
    counts = {"AAPL": 0, "MSFT": 0}

    def buyer(sym: str, price: int) -> None:

        _order, trades = engine.submit_order(sym, Side.BUY, price_cents=price, quantity=10)
        with guard:

        t.join()

# ── CONCURRENCY: the READ path (snapshot) races the WRITE path (submit) ─────────
#
# snapshot() looks read-only but isn't: best_bid/best_ask/is_crossed call
# _peek_active, which heappop()s lazily-deleted (cancelled/filled) dead tops — a
# MUTATION of the same heap list that match() pops from. A monitoring thread
# polling snapshot() therefore shares heap mutation with a submit on the SAME
# symbol. The two tests below (a) prove the shipped engine survives that race and
# (b) prove the per-symbol lock is what makes it survive — i.e. the lock is
# genuinely load-bearing, not decorative.


def _race_snapshot_against_submit(engine: MatchingEngine) -> list[str]:
    # Seed many resting SELLs, then cancel them so they become DEAD tops the
    # purge logic must pop. Crossing BUYs walk _asks popping dead tops in match(),
    # while pollers walk _asks popping the same dead tops in snapshot() -> two
    # threads mutating ONE heap. Returns any exceptions seen (a torn book).
    errors: list[str] = []

    ids: list[str] = []
    for i in range(400):
        order, _ = engine.submit_order(
            "AAPL", Side.SELL, price_cents=1000 + i % 3, quantity=10
        )
        ids.append(order.order_id)
    for order_id in ids:
        engine.cancel_order(order_id)

    stop = threading.Event()

    def poller() -> None:
        while not stop.is_set():
            try:
                engine.snapshot("AAPL")
            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - the whole point is to catch a torn book
                errors.append(repr(exc))
                return

    def submitter() -> None:
        for _ in range(60):
            try:
                engine.submit_order("AAPL", Side.BUY, price_cents=2000, quantity=10)
            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - a torn heap surfaces here too
                errors.append(repr(exc))
                return

    pollers = [threading.Thread(target=poller) for _ in range(6)]
    writer = threading.Thread(target=submitter)
    for p in pollers:
        p.start()
    writer.start()
    writer.join()
    stop.set()
    for p in pollers:
        p.join()

    return errors


def test_snapshot_concurrent_with_submit_does_not_tear_book() -> None:
    # The shipped, locked engine: polling snapshot() while submits flow on the
    # SAME symbol must NEVER tear the heap, because snapshot now takes the same
    # per-symbol lock. No exception, and the book is still consistent afterward.
    engine = ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
    errors = _race_snapshot_against_submit(engine)

    assert errors == [], f"snapshot/submit race tore the book: {errors[:3]}"
    snap = engine.snapshot("AAPL")
    assert snap["crossed"] is False
    assert snap["ask_qty"] >= 0 and snap["bid_qty"] >= 0


def test_per_symbol_lock_is_load_bearing(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    # NEGATIVE CONTROL. We widen the check-then-act window in _peek_active (the
    # heap-mutating purge) with a yield so two threads can both pass the dead-top
    # check and both heappop. With this window open:
    #   - the no-op-lock MatchingEngine TEARS the heap (IndexError) — proving the
    #     race is real and the lock is the only thing preventing it;
    #   - the ThreadSafeMatchingEngine stays clean — proving the lock closes it.
    original_peek = OrderBook._peek_active

    def slow_peek(self: OrderBook, heap: list) -> object:
        while heap and not heap[0].order.is_active:
            time.sleep(0)  # force a thread switch BETWEEN the check and the pop
            heapq.heappop(heap)
        return heap[0].order if heap else None

    monkeypatch.setattr(OrderBook, "_peek_active", slow_peek)
    try:
        # Unlocked core: the widened window must produce a torn book.
        unlocked_errors = _race_snapshot_against_submit(MatchingEngine(["AAPL"]))
        assert unlocked_errors, (
            "expected the unlocked engine to tear under the widened race window; "
            "if this is empty the lock is not actually load-bearing"
        )

        # Same window, but the per-symbol lock serialises the heap mutations.
        locked_errors = _race_snapshot_against_submit(
            ThreadSafeMatchingEngine(["AAPL"])
        )
        assert locked_errors == [], (
            f"the per-symbol lock failed to close the race: {locked_errors[:3]}"
        )
    finally:
        monkeypatch.setattr(OrderBook, "_peek_active", original_peek)
